title: FIPS mode secret always mounted on Fips enabled hosts - optional?
body:
/kind feature

**Description**

When running a container with a Fips aware OS (e.g. RHEL 7/8) on a Fips enabled host (e.g. RHEL 8.3) podman mounts a FIPS mode secret at `/etc/system-fips` and creates a symlink to `/run/secrets/system-fips`.

In many cases this is a prefect behavior!

But in some cases.. when the process (software) is not prepared to handle Fips this leads to trouble.

Concrete Example:

- RHEL 8.3 (Fips mode enabled)
- Podman: 2.0.5
- Container: RH Quay v3.3.4 with RHEL 7.9 

Quay crashes cause some python scripts use md5. 

I found no way to tell podman NOT to mount the FIPS mode secret into the container. 


**Describe the results you received:**

Podman always mounts FIPS mode secret on a fips enabled host.

**Describe the results you expected:**

Podman option to ignore Fips and not mounting FIPS mode secret on a fips enabled host.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Running Quay with podman via systemd. 

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.0.5
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.14.7
Built:        Wed Sep 23 18:18:02 2020
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

No/Yes

comments.body: You should not run non fips mode software on a fips mode enabled system. That is the basic idea of fips mode.  If you are breaking the rules then you are not fips mode.
@rhatdan  I didn't want to be taught. I had a specific question.

Podman only checks for /etc/system-fips on the Host file system to decide is a container running on a fips enabled host. If you rename /etc/system-fips before starting the container, no symlink /run/secrets/system-fips is created and from now the container believes it's not on a fips host. 

I wander cause.... 
```
bash-4.2$ cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled
1
bash-4.2$ sysctl crypto.fips_enabled
crypto.fips_enabled = 1
```
...is still showing fips is enabled.

@rhatdan It's so easy to break the rules... so in my opinion disabling Fips in podman can be a run option too :-) 

Now I ask myself... is checking only for /etc/system-fips a secure way?
Yup,  I guess we could add a --security-opt fips=disable or something like that if you are interested in opening a PR.  :^)
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I have been told that openssl library will only work in fips mode if both the /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled is trueand the /etc/system-fips file exists.  When the /etc/system-fips file exists on the host then we create /run/secrets/system-fips so the containers settings match the hosts.  Then if the machine is in fips mode, the openssl will follow the rules.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we have had no futher response, I am closing this issue.
